support_claim: none; Mixtral support posture unchanged (active validation / partial backend runtime only)

findings.tokenizer: The 2026-05-14 sev1 raw_hello parity failure was a fixture-capture defect, not a runtime defect. Against the SAME artifact that lane pinned (sha cdca4a8c...), current llama.cpp llama-tokenize emits [1, 22557] (' Hello', SPM space prefix applied) for raw 'Hello', and Camelid's encodes_mixtral_real_prompts_like_llama_cpp_when_available passes with the corrected pack (raw_hello = [1, 22557]; the in-template cases were already consistent). Verified on this host with both outputs captured in this bundle.
findings.safe_plan_timing: On a 16GB Apple M4 host, serve places this unpromoted row on the safe cpu_reference plan; a 10-12 token greedy chat could not finish within a 15-minute (then 58-minute) wall clock with file-backed experts, reproducing the recorded long-generation 'hang' class as planner-selected reference-path cost rather than a deadlock. The generation-timeout guard returned controlled 503s as designed (captured response in this bundle).
findings.reference_loadability: Current llama.cpp (brew) refuses to GENERATE with this split-expert tensor naming ('missing tensor blk.0.ffn_down_exps.weight', reproduced locally), matching the sev1 lane's reference failure; any future Gate 9A rerun needs a pinned older llama.cpp or a merged-naming requant on the reference side. Tokenization works.

open_blockers: Gate 9A 50-token generation divergence remains the recorded parity blocker (prior step-logit evidence shows llama.cpp's own decode and forced-prefill paths disagree at the divergence point); file-backed expert generation cost remains the continuation blocker. Both stay open; no posture change.
